But anyway, Mike and Mike Simpson and I talked about this, but after the bill passed, we could both be walking down the street. I mean, this happens to me not irregularly at the Boise Co-op. It happens to him in Idaho Falls. People will stop. Him. And thank him for the boulder. White clouds. I can tell when Outdoor Idaho, the TV show, has aired the Boulder White Clouds story because people will stop me in the in the co-op and they'll say, oh, I saw that show again. And people will watch it over and over again. And, um, it is all these things that I ever did was with a whole bunch of people or as, um, you know, Hillary Clinton used to say, it takes a village. But as a friend of mine with the Forest Service said, yeah, but every village needs a mayor. And I guess I was that mayor. Um, but I was I served because people were had my back. Yeah. And, uh, were to Preserve in perpetuity is a great inspiration. And it is, uh, that is a quote from Howard Zahniser, the person who wrote the Wilderness Act originally, um, ironically moved through the US Senate by Senator Frank church. Um, can you imagine an Idaho politician moving a bill like that now through the US Congress? Yeah. So but working to preserve in perpetuity is a great inspiration, and it is inspiring to do it and make friends. It's even more inspiring to win it. Um, and, uh, I did it for a really long time before I started getting invited to the white House for these bill signings. Um, I mean, I thought Mike Crapo was going to cry at the Owyhee thing because he he didn't he's been in the Senate for a long time. He didn't pass that many bills. Um, let alone how did he think he was going to be passing a conservation bill? Um, he practically disavows it now. Um, but it's it was a these are big things. And, you know, it's it didn't change the world or make world peace or anything, but it's on the roadmap. And people will go there this summer and they will see the sign. This is Cecil Andrus, White Clouds Wilderness or the Jim McClure East Fork Wilderness or what have you. And they will go somebody. I wonder who those people were. I wonder who got this done. And, um, that's that's cool. You know, there there's somebody I've been asking these kind of things, you know, how do you want to be remembered? And is Cecil Andrus? I asked Cecil Andrus that question. He goes, Rick, no, you know, and when I die there, they'll do a nice funeral and my family will be sad. And the people will talk to me about a couple about me for a couple of weeks, but then no one will remember. Now, I think Cecil Anders is a little different than me, but I think that's true because I already can tell you that I could walk into the Idaho Conservation League office right now to pick up my wine this afternoon, and there are people there that don't know who I am. Yeah. And, um, that's just how it goes. But I ran the place for half of its history, you know? So it's it's interesting. And you get comfortable with that. You know, I'm proud of what I did. I'm also very comfortable with letting the water flow by and maybe cast a line or two. Call it good. I think that's a good place to end this one. But thank you, Rick, for taking the time to come down here and talk to me. I love this stuff. Love to love to chat with you again. Uh, I'm very open to that. Um, you know, whether it's this project or your project, your PhD.

lines.line: > The speaker introduces himself as Stephen Austin, a doctoral candidate at the University of Idaho, conducting an oral history interview with Rick Johnson, a former director of the Idaho Conservation League. The interview is part of a project supported by the Idaho Historical Society, focusing on environmental issues and wolf reintroduction in Idaho.

metadata.title: Introduction to the Interview
metadata.synopsis: The speaker introduces himself as Stephen Austin, a doctoral candidate at the University of Idaho, conducting an oral history interview with Rick Johnson, a former director of the Idaho Conservation League. The interview is part of a project supported by the Idaho Historical Society, focusing on environmental issues and wolf reintroduction in Idaho.

lines.line: # Rick Johnson's Early Life and Initial Career

> Rick Johnson shares his background, coming from the East Coast to Idaho with the intention of spending just a summer. His initial plans changed as he became deeply connected with the natural environment and local history, leading him to settle and start a construction business in Idaho.

metadata.title: Rick Johnson's Early Life and Initial Career
metadata.synopsis: Rick Johnson shares his background, coming from the East Coast to Idaho with the intention of spending just a summer. His initial plans changed as he became deeply connected with the natural environment and local history, leading him to settle and start a construction business in Idaho.

lines.line: > The speaker describes his transition from running a construction business to becoming involved in environmental advocacy. His engagement with the local community and historical conservation issues sparked his interest in protecting Idaho's natural landscapes, leading to his deeper involvement in environmental causes.

metadata.title: Transition to Environmental Advocacy
metadata.synopsis: The speaker describes his transition from running a construction business to becoming involved in environmental advocacy. His engagement with the local community and historical conservation issues sparked his interest in protecting Idaho's natural landscapes, leading to his deeper involvement in environmental causes.

lines.line: > Rick Johnson recounts his progression into formal environmental advocacy, beginning with contract work for the Idaho Conservation League and eventually leading to significant political interactions and legislative efforts. His work included lobbying in Washington, D.C., and collaborating with key political figures to advance environmental protection laws.

metadata.title: Political Involvement and Legislative Efforts
metadata.synopsis: Rick Johnson recounts his progression into formal environmental advocacy, beginning with contract work for the Idaho Conservation League and eventually leading to significant political interactions and legislative efforts. His work included lobbying in Washington, D.C., and collaborating with key political figures to advance environmental protection laws.

lines.line: # Challenges and Strategies in Environmental Advocacy

> The speaker discusses the strategic aspects of environmental advocacy, focusing on building relationships and navigating political landscapes to promote conservation. He highlights the importance of aligning conservation efforts with local values and the challenges of working in a conservative state like Idaho.

metadata.title: Challenges and Strategies in Environmental Advocacy
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses the strategic aspects of environmental advocacy, focusing on building relationships and navigating political landscapes to promote conservation. He highlights the importance of aligning conservation efforts with local values and the challenges of working in a conservative state like Idaho.

lines.line: > Rick Johnson reflects on his interactions with various governors and his role in significant environmental campaigns. He emphasizes the importance of perseverance and strategic planning in achieving long-term conservation goals, and he shares insights on the evolving dynamics of environmental advocacy and the inclusion of tribal perspectives.

metadata.title: Reflections on Career and Achievements
metadata.synopsis: Rick Johnson reflects on his interactions with various governors and his role in significant environmental campaigns. He emphasizes the importance of perseverance and strategic planning in achieving long-term conservation goals, and he shares insights on the evolving dynamics of environmental advocacy and the inclusion of tribal perspectives.

# Rick Johnson's Early Life and Initial Career

> Rick Johnson shares his background, coming from the East Coast to Idaho with the intention of spending just a summer. His initial plans changed as he became deeply connected with the natural environment and local history, leading him to settle and start a construction business in Idaho.

His work included lobbying in Washington, D.C., and collaborating with key political figures to advance environmental protection laws.
# Challenges and Strategies in Environmental Advocacy

> The speaker discusses the strategic aspects of environmental advocacy, focusing on building relationships and navigating political landscapes to promote conservation. He highlights the importance of aligning conservation efforts with local values and the challenges of working in a conservative state like Idaho.

> Rick Johnson reflects on his interactions with various governors and his role in significant environmental campaigns. He emphasizes the importance of perseverance and strategic planning in achieving long-term conservation goals, and he shares insights on the evolving dynamics of environmental advocacy and the inclusion of tribal perspectives.

story.title: Rick Johnson - Wolf Reintroduction May 14, 2025
story.description: In this interview, Stephen Austin, a doctoral candidate, interviews Rick Johnson, former director of the Idaho Conservation League, for an oral history project. They discuss Johnson's career, starting from his move from the East Coast to Idaho, his involvement in environmental issues like wilderness preservation, and his work with various state politicians and the federal government. Johnson highlights key moments such as working on the Boulder White Clouds Wilderness and Owyhee Canyonlands, and touches on strategies for building support for conservation in conservative areas. The conversation also covers wolf reintroduction conflicts and the political landscape's effect on environmental advocacy.
